---
title: "Northwestern's closest ML collaborators are in China. And other things I found mapping 26,556 papers."
date: 2026-05-20
description: "I parsed the affiliations off every NeurIPS, CVPR, ICLR, and ICML paper from 2024–2025 and drew the co-authorship graph. Here's what the structure of ML research actually looks like."
categories: [tools, literature, visualization]
image: /assets/lab-network-preview.png
image-alt: "Graph of 476 ML institutions linked by co-authored papers, five colored communities"
page-layout: full
toc: false
draft: true
---

I spent a weekend running a small local LLM over the first page of every
NeurIPS, CVPR, ICLR, and ICML PDF from 2024 and 2025, pulled the author
affiliations off each one, and drew the graph. That's 26,556 unique papers,
476 institutions in the main component, 5,800 co-authorship edges, five
communities by Leiden.

A few things popped out that I didn't expect.

1. A handful of labs end up in the "wrong" community on the graph, and
   each one tells you something. Northwestern, for example, sits with the
   Chinese universities. Its closest co-authors are HKU, Tsinghua, HKUST,
   NTU, Zhejiang. On topic mix it looks like Tsinghua (vision-heavy),
   not like CMU (LLM-balanced).
2. The biggest cross-cluster bridge in the whole graph is CMU ↔ MBZUAI
   (77 papers), and 19% of those joint papers are on causal inference.
   No other top bridge looks like that. Schölkopf's Tübingen-MBZUAI-CMU
   pipeline is right there in the data.
3. Tsinghua is the single largest publisher in the corpus at 1,202 papers,
   ahead of Berkeley (#4, 781) and Stanford (#5, 758). The top 15 has a
   roughly even mix of US, Chinese, European, and Asian institutions.
4. Year-over-year, the biggest movers are Chinese universities and
   NVIDIA (up sharply), and the corporate research labs of the 2018–2022
   era (Google Research, MSR Asia, Tencent AI Lab, all down 25–50%). US
   academia is roughly flat.

The map is below. Click any node to see who it actually collaborates with.
Full screen: [lab_network.html](/lab_network.html).

<iframe
  src="/lab_network.html"
  width="100%"
  height="900"
  style="border:1px solid var(--bs-border-color); border-radius:10px;"
  loading="lazy"
  title="Interactive lab network">
</iframe>

## What's in the data

Same corpus as the [paper map](/posts/paper-map.html): NeurIPS, CVPR, ICLR,
ICML, main tracks only, 2024 and 2025. The previous post showed *what* this
research is about. This one shows *who* is doing it, with whom.

Each node is an institution. Two nodes are connected if they share at least
one accepted paper. Edge weight is the number of joint papers. Communities
are Leiden on the weighted graph. Layout is Fruchterman-Reingold with
community centroids pulled apart so the structure stays legible.

26,556 unique papers. ~24,300 with at least one parsed affiliation. 476
institutions in the main component after dropping the tail below 15 papers.
5,800 co-authorship edges, 2,217 of them crossing community boundaries.
Snapshot from May 2026.

## Why this is worth ten minutes

The graph is most useful if you do one of these things. (a) Pick a dream
lab. The orange edges out of it are the labs that actually share students
and code with it. Those are your real second-choice options. (b) Going to
a conference? The lit-up neighbours of your group are who to grab coffee
with. The dark nodes are cold calls. (c) Writing a grant with four
"international" co-PIs that all sit in one Leiden community? That's not
international. (d) Building a reviewer pool? An AC roster from a single
community will have blind spots.

## The five clusters

Mostly geographic, with one big exception.

- **🔵 US + Big Tech (161 labs).** Berkeley, Stanford, CMU, MIT, DeepMind,
  NVIDIA. The only cluster where academia and industry share one social
  network (~12% industry). DeepMind sits here despite being in London,
  which is the least surprising mismatch on the map.
- **🟠 China + HK / Singapore (155 labs).** Tsinghua, Zhejiang, Peking,
  SJTU, CAS, HKU, NTU Singapore, HKUST. Singapore and Hong Kong
  institutions cluster here by co-authorship, not in a separate Asia
  bucket. Alibaba / ByteDance / Tencent collaborate diffusely, not through
  a few elite pairs the way US industry often does.
- **🟢 Europe (99 labs).** Oxford, Max Planck, ETH, Cambridge, TU Munich,
  UCL, Inria. Almost no industry (2%). Europe's industry ties run through
  DeepMind and US labs, not internally.
- **🔴 Asia / Middle East / Oceania (43 labs).** MBZUAI, Tokyo, Sydney,
  RIKEN, KAUST, ANU. The leftover bin. MBZUAI is the most balanced bridge
  node on the whole map.
- **🟣 Korea (18 labs).** KAIST, SNU, Yonsei, Samsung, Naver. Tight,
  internal. 6 papers bridge to Asia/MidEast, 30 to China, almost
  everything else stays inside.

## The four venues aren't interchangeable

| Rank | CVPR (5,427) | NeurIPS (9,314) | ICLR (5,941) | ICML (5,874) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tsinghua 339 | Tsinghua 479 | Tsinghua 338 | Berkeley 238 |
| 2 | Zhejiang 262 | Peking 352 | Peking 240 | Stanford 162 |
| 3 | Peking 231 | SJTU 326 | CMU 236 | Oxford 125 |
| 4 | SJTU 230 | Zhejiang 311 | Stanford 224 | Google 96 |
| 5 | CAS 220 | CMU 278 | Berkeley 222 | UC Boulder 79 |
| 6 | HKU 194 | HKU 268 | Zhejiang 191 | Zhejiang 79 |
| 7 | Shanghai AI Lab 192 | Stanford 262 | SJTU 190 | Max Planck 68 |
| 8 | NTU Singapore 178 | CAS 259 | DeepMind 183 | Google Research 59 |

CVPR's top of the ranking is dominated by Chinese universities. Tsinghua
alone sits on 6.2% of CVPR papers. Berkeley, the highest-ranked US
institution at CVPR, first appears at #14.

ICML leans the other way: its top 10 is Berkeley, Stanford, Oxford,
Google, UC Boulder, Zhejiang, Max Planck, Google Research, OpenAI,
University of Washington.

DeepMind makes only one venue's top 10 (ICLR). NeurIPS is the most evenly
split. If you want one venue that tracks "global ML", it's NeurIPS.

## Top 15 by accepted papers

| Rank | Institution | Papers |
|---|---|---:|
| 1 | Tsinghua | 1,202 |
| 2 | Zhejiang | 843 |
| 3 | Peking | 827 |
| 4 | UC Berkeley | 781 |
| 5 | Stanford | 758 |
| 6 | SJTU | 749 |
| 7 | CAS | 668 |
| 8 | CMU | 650 |
| 9 | HKU | 619 |
| 10 | HKUST | 591 |
| 11 | NTU Singapore | 571 |
| 12 | Shanghai AI Lab | 548 |
| 13 | Nanjing | 503 |
| 14 | Oxford | 486 |
| 15 | KAIST | 462 |

Tsinghua sits 54% ahead of Berkeley by paper count. Berkeley, Stanford,
CMU, Oxford and KAIST are all in the top 15 alongside the Chinese hubs.

The strongest *internal* edges (joint papers, same cluster): SJTU ↔
Shanghai AI Lab 145, Tsinghua ↔ Shanghai AI Lab 126, Toronto ↔ Vector 121,
Tsinghua ↔ Peking 110, Berkeley ↔ Stanford 101. Shanghai AI Lab appears in
four of the top ten internal edges. It plays a coordinating role in its
cluster that no single US lab plays on the US side.

The strongest *cross-cluster* bridges: CMU ↔ MBZUAI 77, Tsinghua ↔ MSR 37,
Tsinghua ↔ CMU 29, Tsinghua ↔ Berkeley 28, Tsinghua ↔ Stanford 26, MBZUAI
↔ UCSD 25, Tsinghua ↔ Bosch 23. Tsinghua has by far the most external
co-authorship across clusters.

One stat that surprised me: **Zhejiang ↔ Alibaba is 77 papers**, second
only to DeepMind ↔ Google Research in the whole corpus. No US academic ↔
industry pair is close to that volume.

## Labs whose flag doesn't match their color

Flag is where the building is. Color is who they actually publish with.
When the two disagree, you learn something about the lab.

- **🇺🇸 Northwestern (China cluster).** Top co-authors are HKU 12, Tsinghua
  12, HKUST 9, NTU 9, Zhejiang 8, Princeton 8, SJTU 7. Nine out of ten
  are Chinese institutions. Only Princeton is US. By co-authorship,
  Northwestern sits in the China cluster.
- **🇬🇧 DeepMind (US cluster).** London building, Mountain View social
  graph.
- **🇮🇱 Technion / Tel Aviv / Weizmann (US cluster).** Israeli ML is
  structurally a US sub-network. Every senior group I checked has more US
  than local co-authors.
- **🇺🇸 Intel Labs (Europe cluster).** Intel Munich plus ETH / EPFL ties
  dominate its publishing footprint.

## 2024 vs 2025

Splitting the corpus by year shows a few clear movers.

![Risers and fallers, 2024 to 2025. Counts are accepted papers at NeurIPS, CVPR, ICLR, ICML.](/assets/lab-network-delta.png)

Most Chinese universities posted big year-over-year gains. Peking +53%,
CAS +69%, HKU +63%, Nanjing +74%, Sun Yat-sen +83%. Tsinghua a more
modest +29%, but off a much bigger base.

On the industry side, several of the headline 2018–2022 corporate research
labs are publishing less at these venues. Google Research is down 36%
(−86 papers). Tencent AI Lab down 48%. MSR Asia down 27%. SenseTime
down 29%.

NVIDIA is the one industry lab going the other way. +47%, +74 papers. It
now publishes more like an academic group than a corporate one.

Harvard +60% (+86 papers), the biggest US academic gain in absolute terms.
Kempner hires plus a big LLM push.

KAIST +28%, the strongest non-Chinese Asian gain.

The one-liner: US academia is roughly flat, Chinese academia is growing
fast, and corporate research is splitting into NVIDIA up and most others
down.

## What each bridge actually publishes on

The graph tells you who works with whom. It doesn't tell you on what.

To get the topics I joined the affiliations data with the cluster
assignments from [the paper map](/posts/paper-map.html), so each joint
paper carries both an institution pair and a topic label (one of 377
clusters from the embeddings, rolled up into broader themes).

![Topic mix per institution. Vision vs LLMs.](/assets/lab-network-topics-by-inst.png)

The single most surprising plot for me: **Northwestern's topic profile
looks like Tsinghua, not like CMU.** 16% LLMs, 45% vision and 3D. The US
peers (Berkeley, Stanford, CMU) sit near 50/50 on LLMs and vision. Oxford
and MBZUAI tilt heavily to vision. DeepMind is the LLM specialist of the
top labs in the corpus.

Northwestern's "wrong" community label on the graph isn't a glitch. It's
a topic match.

The bridges sharpen the picture:

![Topic mix per bridge. LLM share descending.](/assets/lab-network-topics.png)

- Tsinghua's US bridges are LLM pipelines. Berkeley 56%, Stanford 50%, MSR
  50%, DeepMind/Oxford 44%. If you want to know what a joint NeurIPS paper
  between Tsinghua and a US group looks like in 2025, the honest answer is
  "an LLM paper".
- CMU ↔ MBZUAI is the weirdest top bridge. 24% LLMs and 19% causal
  inference. No other top bridge has that causal share. Schölkopf's
  Tübingen-MBZUAI-CMU axis is right there in the data.
- Northwestern's China bridges are vision, not language. Northwestern ↔
  HKUST is 75% vision and 3D. Northwestern ↔ Tsinghua is 50% vision plus
  17% medical. Near-zero LLM share. That's a SIGGRAPH-adjacent CVPR
  pipeline, not a language model pipeline.
- Shanghai AI Lab is the most balanced node. Its bridges spread across
  LLMs, diffusion, 3D, video and vision in single-digit-percent steps,
  which is why it shows up in four of the top ten internal edges.

The useful version of the Northwestern story: they publish with China on
vision and 3D. If you're a vision PhD heading to HKUST on exchange, you're
walking into an existing pipeline. If you're an LLM PhD, you're cold
calling.

## How to read the picture in 30 seconds

- Five blobs = five social communities, not five countries.
- Bigger dot = more accepted papers.
- Grey edges live inside a community. Orange edges cross communities.
- Edge thickness is the count of shared papers.
- A lab with many orange edges is a bridge institution. Those are where
  ideas move between camps.

## Caveats

The big one: affiliations were parsed by a small local LLM from the first
page of each PDF. It gets the obvious cases right (Stanford, Tsinghua,
DeepMind), but it misses on long footer affiliations, non-standard
transliterations, and papers where authors list four affiliations each. I
added a canonicalization layer (anchor dictionary plus fuzzy merge), but
small labs are still fragmented and the very top labs are probably
inflated by a few percent. I also dropped institutions with fewer than 15
papers to keep the graph readable, which cuts the universe from ~10,000
raw strings down to 483 nodes.

Country tagging is keyed off a hardcoded name → ISO list, so the country
filter is incomplete for the long tail. About a third of nodes have no
country code and won't show up under any country button.

Missing on purpose: workshops, datasets-and-benchmarks tracks, ACL, EMNLP,
MICCAI, ISBI. Same scope as the paper map. Medical imaging venues are
still on the list.

If your lab is missing or merged with someone else's, ping me on
[GitHub](https://github.com/BraveDistribution) with the canonical name
and I'll add it to the anchor list and rerun.
